key_insight: A gradient Al2O3/Cr2O3 detonation-sprayed coating outperformed a bilayer design by becoming denser, harder, more wear-resistant, and more corrosion-resistant.
engineering_relevance: For 316L stainless steel surfaces, gradient ceramic architectures can improve service life in harsh wear-and-corrosion environments by reducing defects and stress concentrations through the coating thickness.
method: Detonation sprayed Al2O3/Cr2O3 coatings were deposited on 316L stainless steel in gradient and bilayer forms.
Microstructure and phase state were assessed with XRD, SEM, and EDS.
Mechanical, tribological, and corrosion behavior were measured using indentation, scratch testing, ball-on-disk, and potentiodynamic polarization in 3.5% NaCl.
evidence_signal: The gradient coating showed lower porosity (0.934% vs 1.285%), higher hardness (~462 vs ~401 HV), lower friction, and lower corrosion current density (0.24155 vs 0.50540 µA/cm2) than the bilayer coating.
watch_item: Performance gains were demonstrated under laboratory test conditions, so long-term durability and scalability under real service environments still need validation.
paper_title: Structural and Phase State and Properties of Gradient and Bilayer Al2O3/Cr2O3 Coatings Obtained by Detonation Spraying
